[Healthy ageing](https://www.whiddon.com.au/yourlife/healthy-ageing/)
Written by Whiddon

# Why Social Support Is Crucial As We Grow Older

The importance of social connections for our overall wellbeing cannot be overstated, particularly as we age. At this stage of our life, we experience numerous changes that impact various aspects of our day-to-day interactions, including our social wellbeing That’s why at Whiddon, our [**aged care services**](https://www.whiddon.com.au/) put relationships at the centre of the care we deliver, ensuring that our residents receive holistic support to support their needs Maintaining these social connections is not just a matter of pleasure but of health and longevity.

## The Impact of Ageing On Social Connections

As we age, our social circles can often change. This can lead to reduced social interaction and, consequently, feelings of loneliness and isolation. The [**World Health Organisation**](https://www.who.int/activities/reducing-social-isolation-and-loneliness-among-older-people) has highlighted the prevalence of social isolation among older adults, noting its potential to affect physical and mental wellbeing. This growing concern highlights the need for focused efforts to help older adults maintain and establish rewarding social connections.

## What Is Social Support?

[**Social support**](https://www.whiddon.com.au/care-solutions/community-care/social-support/) is a network of services designed to help older people remain connected to or re-establish connections with their community of family and friends. This can range from group activities or intimate one-on-one chats with aged care support workers. The [**benefits of robust social support**](https://www.whiddon.com.au/yourlife/why-building-relationships-is-essential-in-aged-care/) include improved mental health, reduced stress, and a more fulfilling and happier life. Social support groups, particularly those tailored for older adults, play a crucial role in providing these benefits.

## How Community Care Can Help

Community care services, such as those offered by Whiddon, are vital in bridging the gap caused by reduced social interactions in older age. Our innovative approach to community care includes a range of services and programs designed to encourage socialisation, engagement, and active participation in community life. This includes social groups and clubs focused on activities that spark interest, such as cooking classes, exercise groups, technology classes and outings to town. Drawing upon our relationship-based care approach, [**MyLife**](https://www.whiddon.com.au/creative-ageing/mylife/), these services not only provide practical assistance but also create exciting opportunities for older adults to create new and meaningful relationships.

## Staying Connected As You Grow Older

At Whiddon, we believe that the role of social support in promoting a healthier and happier ageing experience cannot be underestimated. The need for effective social support structures is more pressing than ever, so we are committed to addressing this need by fostering environments where older adults can thrive socially, emotionally, and physically.

If you or a loved one is interested in finding out about [**Whiddon’s Community Care**](https://www.whiddon.com.au/care-solutions/community-care/) social support services, feel free to get in touch with our team today.

[Talk to our team](/contact/)

## Navigating Elderly Community Support: Essential Programs and Services

#### November 19, 2024

![Elderly community support](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

# Elderly Community Support: 10 Essential Powerful Benefits

**Elderly community support** plays a crucial role in helping aging individuals transition smoothly through different stages of care while continuing to live vibrant and independent lives. For those eager to remain in the comfort of their own homes—often referred to as “aging in place”—these services provide essential assistance. Here’s a quick glimpse of what elderly community support encompasses:

Aging doesn’t have to mean giving up independence. In fact, with the right support structures in place, older adults can enjoy fulfilling lives surrounded by peers and engaging in community events. Organizations like the Older Americans Act Supportive Services and Senior Centers Program help fund the necessary services to keep seniors living comfortably in their own homes.

Over the years, the demand for compassionate, comprehensive caregiving has grown significantly. Recognizing this, both public and private sectors continue to evolve to meet these needs, ensuring aging populations have access to everything from nutritional programs to emergency alert systems.

![Infographic of elderly community support: showing home health services, social connectivity, daily living assistance, transportation, and health - Elderly community support infographic pillar-5-steps](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Infographic of elderly community support: showing home health services, social connectivity, daily living assistance, transportation, and health - Elderly community support infographic pillar-5-steps](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Essential Services for Older Adults

As we age, maintaining independence and quality of life becomes increasingly important. **Elderly community support** provides a range of essential services that help older adults live comfortably and safely. Let’s explore some of the key services available:

### Home Health Care

**Home health care** is crucial for seniors who wish to stay in their own homes. This service includes medical care, like managing medications and monitoring health conditions, and personal care, such as assistance with bathing and dressing. According to [Medicare.gov](https://www.medicare.gov/homehealthcompare), there are numerous Medicare-certified home health agencies across the country, making it easier for seniors to access these services.

### Everyday Activities

For many seniors, daily tasks can become challenging. Assistance with **everyday activities** like cooking, cleaning, and personal hygiene helps them maintain their independence. The Family Caregiver Alliance offers guidance on hiring in-home help to ensure these needs are met effectively.

### Nutrition

Proper nutrition is vital for maintaining health in older age. Programs like the **Elder Farmers Market Nutrition Program** and **Nutritional Education for Older Adults** provide access to fresh produce and nutritional guidance. These initiatives help seniors make healthier food choices, supporting their overall well-being.

### Transportation

Staying mobile is key to an independent lifestyle. Many community services offer **transportation** options for seniors, ensuring they can get to medical appointments, run errands, or simply enjoy a day out. This service is essential for maintaining social connections and accessing necessary resources.

### Emergency Alert Systems

Safety is a top priority for older adults living alone. **Emergency alert systems** provide peace of mind by allowing seniors to call for help at the touch of a button. These systems are crucial in emergencies, ensuring timely assistance when needed.

### Caregiver Support

Supporting caregivers is as important as caring for seniors themselves. Programs like the **National Family Caregiver Support Program** offer resources and respite care, helping caregivers manage their responsibilities without becoming overwhelmed.

### Professional Services

Legal and financial matters can be complex, especially for older adults. **Professional services** such as the **Senior Legal Services & Senior Legal Helpline** and the **Serving Health Insurance Needs of Elders (SHINE)** program offer advice and support, ensuring seniors have access to the information they need to make informed decisions.

Each of these services plays a vital role in helping seniors lead fulfilling lives. By providing comprehensive support, communities can ensure their older residents remain active, healthy, and engaged.

![Elderly community support infographic - Elderly community support infographic 4_facts_emoji_blue](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Elderly community support infographic - Elderly community support infographic 4_facts_emoji_blue](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Types of Social Support for the Elderly

Social support is a critical component of **elderly community support**, ensuring older adults maintain their mental health and overall well-being. Let’s explore the different types of social support available:

### Emotional Support

Emotional support involves providing empathy, care, and understanding to seniors. This type of support can come from family, friends, or community members who offer a listening ear and companionship. Emotional support helps reduce feelings of loneliness and isolation, contributing to better mental health and a higher quality of life for seniors.

### Instrumental Support

Instrumental support refers to practical assistance with daily tasks. This can include help with household chores, transportation, or even managing medications. Community programs often provide transportation services, ensuring seniors can attend medical appointments and social events. The availability of such support allows seniors to maintain independence while feeling supported by their community.

### Informational Support

Informational support involves providing seniors with the knowledge they need to make informed decisions about their health and lifestyle. Programs like the **Benefits Checkup** help seniors find benefits programs that can assist with healthcare, rent, and other needs. Access to accurate information empowers seniors to make choices that improve their quality of life.

### Community Involvement

Community involvement offers seniors opportunities to engage with others, fostering a sense of belonging and purpose. Participating in group activities, such as volunteering or attending educational programs, improves social connections and cognitive function. Assisted living communities, for example, provide numerous opportunities for lifelong learning and social engagement, which are crucial for maintaining cognitive health.

By incorporating these types of support, communities can create an environment where seniors thrive, maintaining their independence and well-being. This leads us to explore the various **community participation opportunities** available for seniors.

## Community Participation Opportunities

Engaging in community activities is vital for seniors to stay active, connected, and fulfilled. Here, we’ll explore several community participation opportunities that improve the lives of older adults.

### Educational Programs

Lifelong learning is not just a buzzword; it’s a reality for many seniors. Educational programs offer older adults a chance to learn new skills, explore interests, and keep their minds sharp. Many senior centers provide classes ranging from technology to arts and crafts. These programs not only stimulate cognitive function but also offer a platform for social interaction and bonding with peers.

### Volunteering

Volunteering provides seniors with a sense of purpose and the opportunity to give back to their communities. By participating in volunteer activities, older adults can share their skills and experiences while making meaningful contributions. This engagement fosters a sense of belonging and can lead to new friendships and connections.

### Hobbies

Pursuing hobbies is another excellent way for seniors to stay engaged and active. Whether it’s gardening, painting, or playing music, hobbies can enrich seniors’ lives and provide a sense of achievement. Participating in hobby groups or clubs can also foster social connections, allowing seniors to share their passions with others.

### Senior Centers

Senior centers are hubs of activity and socialization for older adults. They offer a wide range of services, including meal programs, fitness classes, and wellness seminars. These centers provide a supportive environment where seniors can meet new friends, participate in activities, and access resources custom to their needs. Many centers also offer transportation services, making it easier for seniors to attend events and stay connected with their community.

### Social Benefits

Participating in community activities offers numerous social benefits for seniors. Engaging with others can reduce feelings of loneliness and isolation, leading to improved mental health and well-being. Research shows that regular social interactions can lower the risk of depression and improve cognitive function. By staying active in their communities, seniors can enjoy a more fulfilling and healthy life.

In summary, community participation is essential for seniors’ well-being, offering opportunities for learning, socializing, and contributing to their communities. These activities not only improve quality of life but also foster a sense of belonging and purpose. Now, let’s dig into the benefits of community support for the elderly and how it positively impacts their health and happiness.

## Benefits of Community Support for the Elderly

Community support is a cornerstone of well-being for older adults, offering a range of health benefits that extend beyond just physical care. Let’s explore how **elderly community support** can significantly improve mental and physical health.

### Reduced Depression and Anxiety

Social isolation is a significant risk factor for depression and anxiety among seniors. Engaging with community support services can mitigate these feelings by fostering social connections. Activities like group fitness classes and social gatherings at senior centers provide opportunities for interaction and companionship. As Dr. Eichenberger notes, regular engagement in community activities can lead to “lower perceived levels of stress” and fewer depressive symptoms.

![Regular social interactions can lower the risk of depression and improve cognitive function. - Elderly community support infographic 3_facts_emoji_grey](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Regular social interactions can lower the risk of depression and improve cognitive function. - Elderly community support infographic 3_facts_emoji_grey](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### Improved Cognitive Function

Staying mentally active is crucial for maintaining cognitive health. Educational programs and lifelong learning opportunities available through community support help keep the mind sharp. Participating in classes or workshops stimulates mental faculties and encourages continuous learning. As mentioned in the research, these activities not only increase knowledge but also improve cognitive function, contributing to a healthier brain.

### Reduced Heart Disease and High Blood Pressure

Physical activity is a key factor in reducing the risk of heart disease and managing blood pressure. Community support programs often include fitness classes and wellness programs custom for seniors. Activities such as Tai Chi and walking clubs, which are commonly offered at senior centers, promote cardiovascular health and help in maintaining a healthy lifestyle. Regular exercise is statistically linked to a longer life and a lower risk of developing serious diseases like heart disease.

Community support for the elderly is more than just a safety net; it’s a pathway to a healthier, happier life. With reduced depression and anxiety, improved cognitive function, and better heart health, seniors can enjoy a higher quality of life. Next, we will explore the **key programs and services** that make these benefits possible.

## Key Programs and Services

When it comes to supporting older adults, a range of **[home and community-based programs](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4339950/)** and other services are essential. These programs not only provide practical assistance but also improve the quality of life for seniors.

### Home and Community-Based Programs

**Adult Care Food Program**  
This program ensures that older adults have access to nutritious meals, which is crucial for maintaining good health. By participating, seniors can enjoy balanced diets that support their overall well-being.

**Alzheimer’s Disease Initiative**  
Designed to assist those affected by Alzheimer’s, this initiative offers support to both patients and their families. It includes resources and services aimed at managing the disease and improving the quality of life for those affected.

**Community Care For the Elderly (CCE)**  
CCE provides a variety of services that help seniors live independently in their own homes. These services may include personal care, homemaker services, and case management.

**Elder Farmers Market Nutrition Program**  
This program allows seniors to access fresh, locally grown fruits and vegetables, promoting healthier eating habits and supporting local farmers.

**Emergency Home Energy Assistance Program (EHEAP)**  
EHEAP offers assistance to low-income seniors who need help paying their energy bills, ensuring they stay comfortable and safe in their homes.

**Home Care for the Elderly (HCE)**  
HCE provides financial assistance to caregivers who support elderly individuals in their homes, helping to ease the financial burden of caregiving.

**Medicaid Long-Term Care Services**  
These services offer comprehensive care for seniors who require long-term assistance, covering a range of needs from medical care to personal support.

**Memory Disorder Clinics**  
These clinics specialize in diagnosing and treating memory disorders, offering vital support and resources to seniors and their families.

**National Family Caregiver Support Program**  
This program provides resources and support to family members who care for elderly relatives, helping them manage the demands of caregiving.

**Nutritional Education for Older Adults**  
Educational programs focus on teaching seniors how to maintain a healthy diet, which is crucial for preventing diseases and promoting overall health.

**Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)**  
PACE offers a comprehensive package of medical and social services for seniors, allowing them to receive care while living in their communities.

**Respite for Elders Living in Everyday Families (RELIEF)**  
RELIEF provides temporary relief to caregivers, giving them a much-needed break while ensuring that their loved ones continue to receive quality care.

**Statewide Medicaid Managed Care Long-Term Care Program (SMMC LTC)**  
This program offers long-term care services to eligible seniors, ensuring they receive the necessary support to live independently.

**Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program**  
SNAP helps low-income seniors afford the groceries they need to maintain a healthy diet, reducing food insecurity among older adults.

### Other Programs and Services

**Disaster Preparedness**  
Preparing seniors for emergencies is crucial. This program provides resources and plans to ensure their safety during disasters.

**Elder Abuse Prevention Program**  
Protecting seniors from abuse is a key priority. This program offers education and resources to prevent and address elder abuse.

**Elder Helpline**  
A valuable resource for seniors seeking information and assistance with various services, the helpline connects them to the support they need.

**Insurance, Medicare, and Medicaid**  
Navigating healthcare options can be complex. These programs offer guidance and support to ensure seniors receive the benefits they’re entitled to.

**Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program**  
This program advocates for the rights of seniors in long-term care facilities, ensuring they receive quality care and are treated with respect.

**Nursing Home Services**  
Providing comprehensive care in a residential setting, these services support seniors who require more intensive assistance.

**Public Guardianship**  
For seniors who need help managing their affairs, public guardianship offers legal and financial assistance.

**Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)**  
SCSEP provides job training and employment opportunities for low-income seniors, helping them remain active and engaged in the workforce.

**Senior Legal Services & Senior Legal Helpline**  
These services offer legal assistance to seniors, helping them steer complex legal issues and protect their rights.

**Serving Health Insurance Needs of Elders (SHINE)**  
SHINE provides free, unbiased health insurance counseling to seniors, helping them understand their options and make informed decisions.

**Transportation**  
Reliable transportation is essential for seniors to maintain independence. Programs offer transportation services to help them access medical appointments, shopping, and social activities.

These **key programs and services** are vital for supporting older adults, ensuring they have access to the care and resources they need to thrive. Next, we’ll explore the various volunteer opportunities available for seniors to give back to their communities.

## Volunteer Opportunities for Older Adults

Volunteering offers seniors a chance to stay active, connect with others, and make a positive impact in their communities. Here are some excellent **[volunteer opportunities for older adults](https://www.forbes.com/health/healthy-aging/volunteer-opportunities-for-older-adults/)**:

**Senior Corps**  
Senior Corps is a national program that provides volunteer opportunities for adults aged 55 and older. Volunteers can choose roles that match their skills and interests, from visiting older adults for companionship to mentoring students. This flexibility allows seniors to contribute in a way that fits their lifestyle.

**Local Charities**  
Many local charities welcome senior volunteers. Organizations like Habitat for Humanity offer roles in homebuilding and restoration, while Feeding America needs help with food distribution. These opportunities allow seniors to support causes they care about while staying engaged with their community.

**National Parks**  
U.S. national parks often seek volunteers for various tasks, such as removing invasive plants or serving as tour guides. These roles provide a chance to enjoy the outdoors and contribute to preserving natural spaces. Whether it’s a one-time event or a long-term commitment, seniors can find a role that suits their interests and availability.

**Food Banks**  
Programs like Meals on Wheels rely on volunteers to deliver meals to homebound seniors. This service is crucial for ensuring that older adults receive nutritious meals. Volunteers can choose to help during their lunch hour or commit to regular schedules, making it a flexible option for many seniors.

Volunteering not only benefits the community but also enriches the lives of older adults by providing purpose and social connections. Next, we’ll look at resources available for in-home services to support seniors and their families.

## In-Home Services Resources

For older adults who wish to remain in their homes, **in-home services** provide essential support. These services help with daily tasks, medical needs, and maintaining independence. Here are some key resources to consider:

**Medicare.gov Home Health Compare**  
This tool is a valuable resource for families seeking home health care. It provides detailed information on every Medicare-certified home health agency in the country. By comparing agencies, families can choose the best fit for their loved ones’ needs. [Visit Medicare.gov Home Health Compare](https://www.medicare.gov/care-compare/) for more information.

**National Institute on Aging (NIA)**  
The NIA offers a helpful tip sheet titled “There’s No Place Like Home – For Growing Old.” It provides guidance on how to continue living independently. This resource covers safety tips, home modifications, and ways to stay engaged in the community. It’s an excellent starting point for families planning for the future. [Explore NIA resources](https://www.nia.nih.gov/health/publication/theres-no-place-home-growing-old).

**Family Caregiver Alliance**  
Hiring in-home help can be a complex process. The Family Caregiver Alliance offers a fact sheet to guide families through this journey. It covers important topics like interviewing potential caregivers, checking references, and understanding legal considerations. This resource is essential for making informed decisions about in-home care. [Learn more from the Family Caregiver Alliance](https://www.caregiver.org/resource/hiring-home-help/).

These resources empower families by providing information and tools to support older adults at home. Whether choosing a home health agency or hiring a caregiver, having access to reliable information makes a significant difference. Next, we’ll explore community services that further improve elderly support.

## Community Services Resources

Beyond in-home services, **community services** offer vital support for older adults, helping them stay active and connected. Here are some essential resources:

**National Adult Day Services Association (NADSA)**  
The NADSA is a key resource for finding adult day care centers. These centers provide a safe environment where older adults can engage in social and recreational activities. They also offer health services and personal care, giving caregivers much-needed respite. Whether it’s for a few hours or a full day, adult day services can significantly improve the quality of life for both seniors and their caregivers. [Learn more about NADSA](http://nadsa.org/learn-more/about-adult-day-services/).

**National Respite Network**  
For families in need of temporary relief from caregiving duties, the National Respite Network is an invaluable tool. It helps locate respite care programs across the United States. Respite care can be provided in-home or at a care facility, and it allows caregivers to rest and recharge. This support is crucial in maintaining the well-being of both the caregiver and the older adult. [Find respite care options](https://archrespite.org/).

**Benefits Checkup**  
Navigating the various benefits and assistance programs available can be daunting. Benefits Checkup simplifies this process by helping older adults and their families identify programs that can aid with prescription drugs, healthcare, rent, and utilities. With over 1,650 programs listed, this tool is comprehensive and user-friendly, making it easier to access the support needed to maintain independence and quality of life. [Start your Benefits Checkup](https://www.benefitscheckup.org/).

These community services resources are designed to improve the lives of older adults by providing access to social, recreational, and financial support. By utilizing these resources, seniors can enjoy a more fulfilling and independent lifestyle. Next, we’ll dig into the benefits of community support and how it impacts the well-being of older adults.

## Frequently Asked Questions about Elderly Community Support

### What services do seniors need most?

Older adults often require a range of **essential services** to maintain their health and independence.

**Home Health Care**: This includes medical care, physical therapy, and other health services provided in the comfort of one’s home. It is crucial for managing chronic conditions and recovering from illnesses or surgeries.

**Everyday Activities**: Assistance with daily tasks like cooking, cleaning, and personal care helps seniors live independently.

**Nutrition**: Programs like Meals on Wheels deliver nutritious meals to those who may struggle to cook for themselves, ensuring they receive the necessary nutrients.

**Transportation**: Reliable transportation services are vital for attending medical appointments and running errands, helping seniors remain active in their communities.

**Emergency Alert Systems**: These systems

SUMMARY:
1 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF THE IMPORTANCE OF SOCIAL SUPPORT FOR THE HEALTH AND WELL-BEING OF OLDER ADULTS ISSUE BRIEF SEPTEMBER 2021 2 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF Executive summary Social determinants of health—the characteristics of the environments in which people are born, live, work, play, and worship—affect the health and well-being of people of all ages. It also includes instrumental support, like financial assistance and informational support, such as useful advice or feedback.22 Given the established relationship between loneliness and unfavorable health outcomes, it is notable that many studies have shown that social support relieves older adults’ loneliness and feelings of isolation, thereby improving both psychological and physical well-being. Social Isolation and Loneliness in Older Adults: Opportunities for the Health Care System.

FULL CONTENT:
1 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF THE IMPORTANCE OF SOCIAL SUPPORT FOR THE HEALTH AND WELL-BEING OF OLDER ADULTS ISSUE BRIEF SEPTEMBER 2021 2 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF Executive summary Social determinants of health—the characteristics of the environments in which people are born, live, work, play, and worship—affect the health and well-being of people of all ages. For older adults, social determinants of health can influence both physical and emotional outcomes because these factors impact a wide array of issues ranging from health literacy to health insurance. Social and community context is one type, and it includes factors such as community connection, civic engagement, and discrimination. For older adults, social engagement through volunteering, membership in clubs, and affiliation with religious communities has well-recognized benefits. By contrast, social isolation and loneliness are also features of a person’s social and community context, and these challenges are prevalent and harmful for older adults. Extensive research demonstrates that among older adults, isolation and loneliness elevate the risk of numerous health conditions, hospitalization, and even affect mortality rates. However, social support from family, friends, and neighbors, as well as ongoing social engagement can counteract these risks. Older adults who feel lonely or isolated can improve their social engagement by nurturing existing social networks, joining a group or club, volunteering in the community, and learning how to use social media and other technologies that facilitate communication. In addition, older adults can also look to healthcare providers, local religious groups, county-level departments of aging, and the local area agency on aging for help and information about optimizing social engagement and social support. 3 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF Social determinants of health and social support Factors such as income, walkable neighborhoods, and educational attainment are examples of social determinants of health1, and these factors are frequently grouped into five categories: • Economic stability • Education access and quality • Healthcare access and quality • Neighborhood and the built environment • Social and community context Social determinants of health can impact older adults’ health in many ways. 2 3,4 ,5 This issue brief focuses on the impact of social support—a key aspect of social and community context–on health behaviors, risk factors, and outcomes in older adults.
The importance of social and community context Social and community context refers to the circumstances in which people live, learn, work, and play, and can impact health and well-being. Examples of these characteristics include cohesion with communities, civic engagement, discrimination, and incarceration.1,6 Each of these aspects of social and community context have been shown to influence health, including the health of older adults. For example, volunteering and civic engagement have well-recognized benefits for older adults, even among seniors with physical and cognitive limitations. 7,8 ,9 By contrast, discrimination and incarceration have very unfavorable health effects on older adults and people of all ages.10 ,11 ,12 ,13 The prevalence of social isolation among older adults Social isolation and loneliness are features of a person’s social and community context, and these challenges are especially prevalent among older adults. According to a report from the National Academies of Science, Engineering, and Medicine, 24 percent of community-dwelling adults aged 65 and older were socially isolated, and four percent were severely socially isolated. The report also revealed that 43 percent of adults 60 and over reported feeling lonely, and of these, 13 percent reported feeling this way often, findings that echo other research demonstrating the severity of social isolation for the aging population. 14 ,15 ,16 4 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF The impact of social isolation on the health and well-being of older adults Research demonstrates that strong social relationships have a favorable impact on key health indicators. For example, a meta-analysis of 148 studies on the relationship between social relationships and mortality showed a 50 percent increased likelihood of survival among participants with strong social relationships.17 Conversely, there is also extensive research demonstrating that social isolation and loneliness are associated with elevated risk of developing numerous health conditions, hospitalization, and excess mortality. Loneliness also impacts physical health, with one study showing that the risk of functional decline among lonely, older adults increase by 59 percent.16 Loneliness contributes to less favorable outcomes among older adults with established health conditions, including common chronic conditions like cardiovascular disease. Social isolation and loneliness are associated with a 29 percent increased risk of developing heart disease and a 32 percent increased risk of stroke.20 It follows that among people with heart disease, loneliness also contributes to increased healthcare utilization, with one study showing that heart failure patients with a high degree of social isolation had a 68 percent increased risk of hospitalization.21 The same study reported that these patients were at nearly four times the risk of death as those who did not experience social isolation.21 A meta-analysis of 19 studies on the association between social relationships and dementia risk showed that less frequent social contact increased the risk of dementia and loneliness by nearly 60 percent. 18, 19 91% were more likely to have depression. 21% were more likely to have generalized depression.
35% more likely to have suicide ideation.
For example, one study of more than 15,000 adults showed that among those who reported being lonely: 5 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF How social support helps older adults Social support is the actual or perceived availability of resources from others, typically one’s social network—family, friends, neighbors, and others.14 Social support includes intangible resources, such as emotional support from a trusted friend or confidant. It also includes instrumental support, like financial assistance and informational support, such as useful advice or feedback.22 Given the established relationship between loneliness and unfavorable health outcomes, it is notable that many studies have shown that social support relieves older adults’ loneliness and feelings of isolation, thereby improving both psychological and physical well-being. 23 ,24 ,25 ,26 Addressing social isolation and enhancing social support There are many ways that older adults who feel lonely or isolated can work to improve their level of social engagement. Some strategies include: Nurturing existing social networks: Maintaining personal relationships with family, friends, and neighbors through calls, visits, walking, or other activities helps to prevent isolation and loneliness.
Joining a group or club: Becoming part of a book group, garden club, or other group-based hobby helps older adults meet new people, form new ties, and build social support.
Volunteering in the community: Many older adults benefit from volunteering their time, skills, and knowledge to an array of community-based organizations.
Offering family assistance: Some older adults can form stronger bonds with family members through babysitting or providing caregiving support.
Going back to work: Retirement can be a stressful event for many older adults because it removes both regular contact with colleagues and a sense of routine. Some older adults respond to these challenges by finding part-time work or nurturing a second career.
Learning how to use social media and other technology: Computers have greatly facilitated the ability of older adults to have video calls with children and grandchildren, and social media allows seniors to remain actively involved in the lives of their friends and family through engagement on these platforms.
6 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF Where to go for help There are many resources that can help older adults enhance their social support networks.
Healthcare providers Older adults should discuss their mental health concerns, including social isolation, with their healthcare team and ask for suggestions on how to address the problem. Local religious groups and organizations Seniors who are interested in building social support in a religious context can contact their local church, synagogue, or mosque to identify groups for retired congregants or other gatherings that are relevant for older adults.
County-level departments of aging Many counties have offices that support an array of recreational, social, and volunteer programs for older adults.
Area Agencies on Aging These agencies are designated by states to address the needs of older adults at the regional or local level, including ideas and resources to prevent loneliness and isolation.
Advocacy organizations The National Council on Aging, AARP, and other organizations that advocate for the well-being of older adults have resources to help address social isolation.
7 PAN FOUNDATION | ISSUE BRIEF Conclusion Social determinants of health encompass a wide array of factors that impact older adults’ health and well-being. The effects can be negative—such as social isolation increasing the risk of developing health conditions and hospitalization. But they can also be positive–such as the favorable impact of social engagement on psychological and physical function in older adults. There are many strategies that can enhance social engagement, including joining clubs or other community-based groups, and there are also local resources that can provide information and guidance on what opportunities are available. As the U.S. population continues to age, it will be increasingly important to ensure that older adults have the knowledge of, and capacity to access opportunities for social engagement, and that these opportunities continue to be supported at the local, state, and federal levels. The PAN Foundation is an independent, national 501 (c)(3) organization dedicated to helping underinsured people with life-threatening, chronic, and rare diseases get the medications and treatments they need by assisting with their out-of-pocket costs and advocating for improved access and affordability.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Strong social support networks significantly improve mental health, reducing stress and depression, and fostering a sense of belonging among older adults.
Community support services empower older adults to live independently, providing essential assistance with daily tasks, enhancing their autonomy and quality of life.
Social support facilitates easier access to healthcare, nutrition programs, and community services, ensuring older adults receive vital resources for healthy living.
Engagement in community activities fosters relationships, strengthens bonds among seniors, and creates a supportive network that enhances overall well-being.
Regular social interactions combat loneliness, promoting emotional health and reducing the risk of mental health issues among aging individuals.
